speaker: interviewer
text: Introduce your family to us, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is Zvi, my husband. This is-- unfortunately, my oldest daughter is not here, Caroline with her son, Alex, who's four. And then this is Jackie with her two children, Lisa and Eric. Lisa is 11 and Eric is seven. And then that is Sylvia with her two girls, Sophie and baby Ella.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sophie is two.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three next month.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll ask you, Zvi. Is there anything you would like to say to Eva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I don't know what you've said in your previous interviews, but [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think you've been very strong. That's how you were able to survive. And you've continued to be very strong and a great tower of strength for everybody since I married you. And which is sometimes a bit problematical. Sometimes not so easy to be married to somebody who's really strong. But, on the whole, it's a great experience. And I think that's probably a trait that has come into many survivors, this strength of willpower. So-- and has to take the rough with the smooth. And we're very lucky that we have such lovely grandchildren and children which are-- I was saying to Tony before that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] our whole generation, I was very lucky. I wasn't in a concentration camp. But, nevertheless, our whole generation has been uprooted. And as a result of the experiences through which all of us have gone, we have some psychological problems, all of us, of one kind or another. And our anchor to the future is our children and grandchildren. Hopefully they'll find back into normality. I think that's all I need to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: How's it been for you, Jackie, growing up with two survivor parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, really, as children we didn't know directly what my mother had been through. And only really as we were getting older that things were coming out and we realized what a strong person she was and how wonderful it was that she'd survived. And, oh, and the little things that she did then we realized why she did them even.

speaker: interviewer
text: For example?

speaker: interviewee
text: Things like when we weren't feeling very well, she used to be quite sort of firm and sort of say, well, you know, snap out of it. You'll be all right. Don't make a big fuss about nothing. And, in fact, I've actually become a bit like that with my children, which I don't think is such a terrible thing, as long as I know when they're not feeling well. But things like that would crop up and then we would sort of almost excuse her and say, well, you know, she have a terrible time. I mean, but it's probably made us the way we are as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sylvia, how do you think having a mother as a survivor has influenced your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as Jackie said, you know, it's made a big impression on us. You know, having a very strong character for a mother has its advantages and disadvantages. And sometimes I feel that, you know I may be trying to live up to this role model, as one does anyway, but with that extra-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the extra sort of background that she's been through. It makes it even more difficult, really. And, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you know, I do think that it's sometimes difficult to say, you know, what would life have been like otherwise. You don't know, do you, what the influences of the Holocaust were and, you know, how it would have been different otherwise. It's difficult to tell from that aspect, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to talk with your mother about her experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think since she wrote the book it's been easier because she's gone through that experience herself, to have cathartic experience. But before that she told us sort of anecdotes and stories, but it was almost like it was someone else's experiences, not her own.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a certain time where she didn't want to talk about it and was protecting us. And then when she did start properly with the book, sort of bringing it all back to her more, and we wanted to know more as well. And she wanted to tell us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you'd like to say now for your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's, you know, like I told you before, that my father always said, we'll live on in our children and in grandchildren. And this is my-- you know, my hope for the future. And I love them very much. And I think I spoil them more than I spoiled my own children. I've become softer a bit. But my husband doesn't agree. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the children, yes. [LAUGHING] Not to the husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the children, yes. And, you know, I'll-- I think they're marvelous. They're lovely. We love each other all very much. Don't we, children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something you'd like to say to Grandma? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Sophie will saying something. You want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: For once she's stunned. [LAUGHS] [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Oma. Yes, huh? I'm called Oma. [LAUGHING] And Eric? You love your Oma?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for coming along this afternoon and being with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're continuing with tape five with Eva Schloss. We stopped the tape because her family came and it was a convenient time for them to come. And they wouldn't have been able to come at the end of the video. So, Eva, we were just talking at the end of tape four that you'd met up with your cousin Mini.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother's cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was my mother's cousin Mini. And she worked-- through her husband who knew Mengele. He got her a job. She was a nurse. She used to help her husband. And so Mengele got her a job as a nurse with him, which was a mixed blessing, of course. But so there she was, just doing something. And we just came to see for my boil. And it was just unbelievable. Was really unbelievable. And she had better treatment and she could get food. And so very often she helped us and bought us some food and encouraged us. And she knew a bit news from the outside world. And it was a great, great help. And, again, you know, if we wouldn't have met her, I certainly wouldn't be here because with little things which made all the difference to survive or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of little things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like bringing us food. And she did give me medicine, you know, which normally they wouldn't hand out. And mainly it was food. And, well, later I'll tell a story where you will see why she really saved us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go into hospital? Were you hospitalized?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never stayed in the hospital there. No, no. She just gave me some cream or whatever for the boil. And it-- actually, it still didn't burst. It was really a terrible boil. And one day when I stood uphill it was-- I was very uncomfortable. I think it was hot as well. And the boil hurt me. And I moved there-- because you are supposed to stand completely still. And I kind of moved around. I even tried to sit on the floor or something. And a kapo saw that and she pulled me by my shoulder and gave me a whack on to my neck. And the boil burst into her hand. And she was furious and she gave me some more kicks and beat me. But at least my boil was-- [LAUGHS] was broken. So that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we-- Mini came around to our block sometimes and we had a chat. And then when we were in Kanada, we had to open fur coats, because people hid cigarettes there or gold or coins or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so Mini said to me, you know, what you have to see if you can find a silver spoon for me. She was from a very wealthy family and so she had some privileges in the camp. We still had terrible tin spoons, rusty and so on. And she said I would just love to eat my soup with a silver spoon. And, of course, you could ask for anything. Eventually you'd find it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, OK. So when you enter the camp there was a-- where Kanada was-- this was a huge compound where all the goods from people had come. And we had to sort through. There's a very infamous picture of heaps and heaps of glasses, for instance, which that was all done in Kanada. All the glasses were collected. And everything had to be sorted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so when we entered the camps they didn't want, of course, us to bring things into the camp, even jewelry, diamonds we could have smuggled in if, you know, if-- so there was this search. Sometimes it was thorough, sometimes it was not so thorough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought oh gosh, I have to smuggle this spoon through the camp. Again, thinking later it was a most stupid thing to do, because if they would have found it on me, they would probably have taken me to the selection and they would have killed me straight away. That's what they did usually if they found things. I mean, as well as to prevent, because if there wouldn't be a punishment, people would smuggle things. So after a few days I found a very nice silver spoon and I put it in my shoe and walked on it into the camp. And, luckily, I got through and nothing happened. And I gave it to Mini. And she was thrilled and kept that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually we had once a week a shower. That was really more or less the only washing you could do. And you entered again one side, got out the other side. And everything you had, your clothes, which you got used to by that week, your shoes, you lost again and you got a new set of whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we got soup in the camp, in the front-- uphill we got our soup. We stood in rows of five. The front had a mug or a bowl and the food was put in there. And the five for few shared that. So if you was the last, you sometimes got nothing. But if you had your own mug, you could hold it out, and you got your own portion of soup. So very often we tried to swap during the week a portion of bread for a mug so that you had-- but by the end of that week when you had your shower, you lost your mug. So it was a very precarious balance. You want to risk losing your ration of bread for a few days-- a mug to get a bit of soup. So sometimes we had a-- wanted a toothbrush or a-- Poles got parcels. Food parcels, they were allowed. And as well little objects or, you know. There was things like this. Was comb and hankies that existed in the camp, but only from parcels which came in. Where'd the parcels come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Poland. I mean, there were Poles, like the communists. And they were allowed parcels, only, and they were allowed to write. There were different treatment for different people. And on their uniforms you had your number and you had a triangle. Different colors. Red, yellow, black, blue. And that for the different groups. Black was for political and red was for criminals and whatever. So everybody knew who people were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yellow, I think. But, you know, I can't really remember. just remember all the different colors. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And we never got for instance any milk or sugar or anything sweet. And I had a terrible craving, the body, as it was, wanted that sugar. And one day I was walking in the camp and I saw something shiny and realized it was crumbs of little crystal bits of sugar. And I went like this and ate it up. Because it was-- as I say, Pole probably had some sugar and might have swapped a bit with somebody and a few crumbs got dropped. And as well at the end of the-- there was a little hut in each block, and the Poles sometimes cooked some food there. Again, they got some extra food and-- from the parcel. And the smell of it drove us mad. And we always went there and tried if they were perhaps kind enough to give us some scraps of something or other. And once I had a mug and they just cooked some potato soup, and I asked them if they couldn't give me the water. And she was in a good mood so she said, yes. Give me your mug. And she poured some water where they had put potatoes in. And I drank that and I thought it was just the most delicious food. And I said to my mother, how stupid we were that we never used that at home. We threw that away. And I decided I was going to keep that later for-- to keep it-- make soup out of it. But, of course, later I realized it really didn't have any taste at all. But my children might say I might given them food which wasn't really very tasteful because, you know, I was hard on them this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after-- no, when we were in Kanada, that was-- they needed a lot more people to work in Kanada because that was June '44, and by that time the Hungarian Jews were taken in. Because till then the Hungarian Jews were protected by the government. But then-- I don't know. They couldn't keep them anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And daily thousands and thousands of Hungarian Jews came, mainly young people. And we saw them pass. Kanada is at the end where the trains come and then they walk past there. And we saw them walk all of those young women-- the men went, of course, somewhere-- and straight to the crematorium, straight to the gas chambers. All those people didn't get into the camp at all. And they brought a lot of belongings because they had told them in the-- because by that time the food was getting very short for the Germans. And they wanted to get as much food as possible, so they had-- each transport had to bring many, many wagons of-- load carloads of bread and carloads of jam and whatever. And that all came through Kanada. It was sorted out in different transports to go back into Germany. But we never got anything, all this food. Of course, in Kanada, yes, we could-- when we could eat. If not the German or a kapo was just walking past, we-- that was one of the reason why it was good to work there because we ate the whole day, really. Bits of chocolate, bit of cheese, pieces of bread. Whatever we found, we ate. And one day-- we had a lunch break as well, which wasn't in any other commando. I was just walking there with my mother and there was a barbed wire where we saw men working. And I looked because men was something we didn't come across in the camp, otherwise prisoners. And I saw somebody who, from the distance, looked very familiar. And I realized it was my father in a striped uniform and a shaved head, of course, with a beret. And I don't know, actually, what he was doing there. But he worked there. And I called out through the barbed wire and he turned and we were flabbergasted. And we went to the fence and said, oh, you are here. And, you know, that you got through, because he knew from the selection.

speaker: interviewer
text: And asked where's Heinz? My mother was a bit somewhere else so she didn't see that. Heinz, he said, is all right. He works in a vegetable garden because they grew things for the Germans. And I said I work in a timber factory and I'm quite good with the boss. So he had worked himself into a fairly decent position. And he said, I see your work in Kanada. Can you get a cigarette? So I said, but you don't smoke. So he said, no, no. Not for me. But cigarettes are worth a lot. You can change them for all kind of things. So I said, well, yes. I'll see. But how can I get something? He said, well, just try to come to the fence and we'll see. So then after the lunch break I went to look up my mother and I said, I've seen [NON-ENGLISH] and says, oh, it's impossible. And he said he'll try to be there again the next day. So the next day we went back to this part of the fence and, indeed, he did come. And that was, of course, wonderful that, you know, we could talk and we knew that all were all right still. And I did find as well in blankets-- we did find whole long packets of cigarettes. And when we-- next time when I saw him, I said, well, what do? He said, well, just throw it over the fence. Which we did and a Nazi saw it, but somehow they let it pass. Sometimes they were-- and in Kanada in general they were much easier going.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had as well-- we were-- before we went back into the camp we had to have a shower in Kanada because we were kept much cleaner and it was very dusty there, you know, from-- they open blankets and all that stuff. And it was hot, so we all had to have showers there before we went back into camp. And it was sort of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] open, but there were planks around it. And the Germans were kind of coming in and tickling us and playing games with us and, you know, as if we were humans. Must stay, they were mostly younger people and better looking people there working in Kanada. And sometimes there were-- once I had-- it was terrible. One kind of-- and they didn't have really women there, so some of the Nazis were quite desperate as well to have sex with women. And, of course, it was very forbidden. God, if they would have been caught, it would have been terrible for them. But, anyway, there was one Nazi who kind of followed me around always. And I was really scared because I thought if I say no [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] God knows what he'll do. So I-- you know, I'll just have to go along if he wants me. So-- and he was in the shower he always followed me. And when I walked from one place to the other. But he had to be just as careful as I had to be. And so one day he kind of really followed me around. And I thought about this will be the end. And then there was a big heap of clothes. And while he was just a bit slow behind me, I kind of jumped under the clothes and dig back under and he didn't see me. And then, somehow, I escaped that time. But that was a big problem for me. I had to really always be careful that I don't get-- and that's what did happen in Kanada. But luckily nothing happened to me. And after about six weeks the transport of the Hungarians was finished and they didn't need so many people and we were dismissed. And then we had to do terrible work. At one time I had to carry big boulders of stones to one side of the camp and another group had to carry it to the other side. And then once we had-- we got all a hammer and we had to make those big stones into small stones. But it was practically impossible because was a small hammer and very hard stones. So, and the Germans stood there and if we didn't hammer all the time, they hit us. And was awful work. And my mother very often tells me now when she walked behind me and she saw me carrying those heavy stones and she saw how I was bent, she thought, oh, my poor little Eva. She is a child. Already she has to do this horrible work. And, you know, she felt awful about it. And she gave me some of her food rations and I suppose she suffered more than I did because she realized, you know, how terrible-- I mean, I did realize as well, but for me it was I wouldn't say an adventure, but it was not as terrible as she felt about it. I even said to her once-- I said to her, you know, if I would know I would come out alive out of this, I would even think it's quite interesting. Because, you know, I was a young girl and there was always something going on. And after being in hiding for two years where I'd been very frustrated because I was very full of energy and there at least I could work and I could do things. I was outside. I mean, it was terrible. But, nevertheless, I said this to her. And but of course I was always afraid of dying. This was always behind me. And this was, of course, a terrible part. And we were always hungry. That was the other-- this was the two things which plagued us continuously. And my mother was quite clever as well. There was a kitchen where they cooked the soup and this food which-- I mean, as I say, sometimes we got some soup. And the Poles got more food than us, and they threw away their-- their bad cabbage and the tops of carrots and rotten vegetable and so on. And we went there and [INAUDIBLE] the tops of carrot. We took that. And they had sort of a big marrow which they didn't peel very thoroughly and we cut off the underneath. And then we went back to the barracks and we said we have parsley to sell, which is rich in vitamin. And we have also vitamin bits. And so we swapped that for bit of bread, of a toothbrush, of a bit of soap, and things like that. So we had a little business going. And then sometimes we found old rags, old hankies, which were discarded, and we took those and we tried to-- in the shower we tried to smuggle them in and wash them a bit. And then we sold it as well for material, which people did want. So, you know, we got a bit extra sometimes. But, nevertheless, my mother always worrying that I would be all right. She became very, very thin. And she was quite tall. And as well she was quite weak after several months of this hard labor. And one day after the shower we noticed that the kapos were kind of shouting more and was a lot of commotion going on. And we knew that very often after showers there were selections going on. So when we came out of the shower, we saw the Nazis, and Mengele was standing there. And we, naked, of course, had to turn, walk past him, turn around, and walk on. And he was saying which side to go. And I went in front and he put me to one side. Then my mother followed and-- and he said to the other side. And I wanted to run to her, to kiss her, and the kapo went and with a big stick beat us both on the back and we were separated. And I thought this was the last I'll ever see my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop here.
